audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Exodus / Shemot covenant ordinances text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Hebrew servant laws, female servant protections, violence penalties, lex talionis, injury liability, and property/animal laws. Strong human-dignity safeguards required.

verses.restored: And these are the judgments/ordinances that you shall place before them.
When you buy a Hebrew servant, six years he shall serve, and in seventh he shall go out free, without payment.
If he comes in by himself, he shall go out by himself; if husband of a woman, his woman/wife shall go out with him.
If his master gives him a woman/wife and she bears him sons or daughters, the woman and her children shall belong to her master, and he shall go out by himself.
But if the servant clearly says, I love my master, my woman/wife, and my children; I will not go out free,
then his master shall bring him to the Divine; he shall bring him to door or doorpost, and his master shall pierce his ear with awl, and he shall serve him into the age.
When a man sells his daughter as female servant, she shall not go out as male servants go out.

If she is harmful/displeasing in eyes of her master who designated her for himself, then he shall let her be redeemed; he has no authority to sell her to foreign people, because he has dealt treacherously with her.
If he designates her for his son, he shall do to her according to judgment/right of daughters.
If he takes another for himself, her food, clothing, and marital rights he shall not diminish.
If he does not do these three for her, then she shall go out for nothing, without silver.
One striking a man so that he dies shall surely be put to death.
But one who did not lie in wait, but Divine let it happen to his hand, I will appoint for you a place where he may flee.
But when a man acts arrogantly against his companion to kill him with cunning, from my altar you shall take him to die.
One striking father or mother shall surely be put to death.
One stealing a human and selling him, or he is found in his hand, shall surely be put to death.
One cursing father or mother shall surely be put to death.
When men quarrel and one strikes his companion with stone or fist, and he does not die but falls to bed,
if he rises and walks outside on his staff, the striker shall be cleared; only his sitting/time lost he shall pay, and healing he shall surely heal.
When a man strikes his male servant or female servant with rod, and the servant dies under his hand, avenging he shall be avenged.
Yet if the servant stands a day or two, he shall not be avenged, for he is his silver.
When men fight and strike a pregnant woman and her children go out, but no harm follows, he shall surely be fined as the woman's husband lays upon him, and he shall give by judges.
But if harm follows, then you shall give life for life,
eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,
burn for burn, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.
When a man strikes eye of his male servant or eye of his female servant and destroys it, he shall send the servant away free for the eye.
And if he knocks out tooth of his male servant or tooth of his female servant, he shall send the servant away free for the tooth.
When an ox gores a man or woman and that one dies, stoning, the ox shall be stoned, and its flesh shall not be eaten; owner of ox is clear.
But if ox was goring previously, and its owner was warned but did not keep it in, and it killed man or woman, the ox shall be stoned and its owner also shall be put to death.
If ransom is laid upon him, he shall give redemption of his life/soul according to all laid upon him.
Whether it gores a son or gores a daughter, according to this judgment it shall be done to him.
If the ox gores a male servant or female servant, he shall give thirty shekels of silver to their master, and the ox shall be stoned.
When a man opens a pit, or when a man digs a pit and does not cover it, and an ox or donkey falls there,
owner of pit shall make restitution; silver he shall return to its owner, and dead animal shall be his.
When one man's ox strikes ox of his companion so that it dies, they shall sell live ox and divide silver, and also divide dead animal.
Or if it was known that the ox was goring previously, and its owner did not keep it in, repaying, he shall repay ox for ox, and dead animal shall be his.

translation_notes: These are ancient covenant ordinances within a slaveholding society; preserve source wording while refusing to normalize slavery as moral ideal.
Hebrew servant release in seventh year partially limits debt-servitude, but does not erase the injustice of ownership systems.
Female servant laws contain protections against sale, deprivation, and abandonment but remain patriarchal and require ethical audit.
Human theft/kidnapping is a capital offense, directly undercutting chattel-slavery logic.
Lex talionis likely functions as proportional justice limitation rather than vengeance escalation.
Verses 20-21 are morally troubling and must be flagged; verse 26-27 grants freedom for bodily injury to servants.
safety_note: Do not use Exodus 21 to justify slavery, patriarchy, domestic violence, or corporal punishment. This is ancient legal material requiring historical-critical and ethical framing. The restored layer preserves the text; application must be governed by liberation, human dignity, anti-domination, and the broader trajectory of release from bondage.
